narrative: Dear Alex, I know this rejection letter stings—pouring your heart into law school applications only to face this setback feels deeply personal, and I'm here to walk you through why our prediction model saw your path to the bar exam the way it did, like piecing together chapters of your story that raised some red flags. At the heart of it, your Year 3 grades landing you in the 5th decile at school stood out as the strongest signal pulling toward a tougher road ahead, suggesting the rigors of advanced legal studies might have been a steeper climb than for many peers. This was amplified by your Year 1 decile of 10th, which, while a strong start, didn't hold steady, painting a picture of momentum that faded over time and nudged the odds further toward potential failure. Your upper-middle family income, though supportive, factored in as a notable influence in that direction too, perhaps reflecting patterns where that background correlates with unique pressures or distractions in high-stakes legal training. Layered on top was your LSAT score of 37, which the model saw as tilting against success, alongside your white racial background and undergraduate GPA of 2.8, both contributing their weight toward those concerns. Being full-time in your studies added another pull in that direction, as if the intensity without balance tipped the scales. Your gender as male offered a small countercurrent, gently favoring passing based on broader trends we've seen. Together, these elements wove a narrative where the model estimated a 56% chance of not passing the bar, not as a judgment on your character or drive, but as a cautious forecast to protect you from investing in a path stacked against you. It's not the end of your story—many have rewritten similar chapters by targeting schools that align better with their trajectory or bolstering those mid-program grades. You've got resilience in your corner; consider this a pivot point to build an even stronger case next time. Hang in there.
